name: El Soplao and Oyambre Park Loop
name_original: Celis, San Vicente de la Barquera, Beilva – circular por el Parque Natural de Oyambre
description: This difficult 29.1-mile road cycling loop through Oyambre Natural Park features 3241 feet of elevation gain and stunning coastal views.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Rionansa are set within the Nansa River Valley in western Cantabria, Spain. This mountainous region features deep valleys and varied terrain, providing diverse elevation changes for cyclists. The area is characterized by a verdant Atlantic climate, offering scenic backdrops for road cycling. Its proximity to the Picos de Europa and the Saja-Besaya Natural Park ensures access to challenging climbs and extensive natural beauty. Best road cycling routes around Rionansa The most popular road cycling route is Piedrasluengas and Collado de Hoz Loop, a 65.2 miles (105.0 km) trail that takes 5 hours 55 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gain through mountainous landscapes. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Collado de Hoz Loop, a difficult 30.4 miles (48.9 km) path. This route offers sustained climbs and descents characteristic of the region's terrain. Local road cyclists also love the El Soplao and Oyambre Park Loop, a 29.1 miles (46.8 km) trail leading through varied Cantabrian landscapes, often completed in about 2 hours 46 minutes. Road cycling around Rionansa is defined by mountainous terrain, river valleys, and low traffic density. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to difficult. The routes in Rionansa are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 40 reviews. More than 2,700 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Rionansa's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Rionansa?
A: There are over 50 road cycling routes available around Rionansa, offering a wide range of options for different skill levels. The komoot community has explored these routes over 2,700 times.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Rionansa?
A: Road cycling routes in Rionansa are characterized by mountainous terrain and deep river valleys, particularly the Nansa River Valley. You can expect varied elevation changes, from sustained climbs and descents to more rolling sections, all set against a verdant Atlantic climate. The region's low traffic density enhances the cycling experience.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Rionansa?
A: The region experiences an Atlantic climate, which generally means vibrant green scenery. While specific weather patterns vary, the spring and autumn months typically offer pleasant temperatures and less extreme weather, making them ideal for exploring the diverse terrain. Summer can also be excellent, though some days might be warmer.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Rionansa?
A: While many routes in Rionansa are moderate to difficult, there are a few easier options available. These routes typically feature gentler gradients, allowing beginners to enjoy the scenic landscapes without excessive challenge. For example, the area around the Saja-Besaya Natural Park offers routes suitable for various fitness levels.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling routes in the Rionansa area?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, Rionansa offers several difficult routes with significant elevation gain. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1004542538/piedrasluengas-and-collado-de-hoz-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Piedrasluengas and Collado de Hoz Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a demanding 65.2-mile route with over 2,300 meters of climbing. Another challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1005123905/collado-de-hoz-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Collado de Hoz Loop&lt;/a&gt;, a 30.4-mile path featuring sustained climbs and descents.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in Rionansa?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around Rionansa are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1004542538/piedrasluengas-and-collado-de-hoz-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Piedrasluengas and Collado de Hoz Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1004536135/el-soplao-and-oyambre-park-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Soplao and Oyambre Park Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which takes you through varied Cantabrian landscapes.

Q: What notable landmarks or viewpoints can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: Many routes offer stunning views and pass by interesting landmarks. You might encounter natural monuments like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5406017" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Oyambre Natural Park&lt;/a&gt; or enjoy panoramic vistas from the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/861319" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santa Catalina Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;. Some routes also lead past cultural sites such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/333494" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Vicente de la Barquera&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4131895" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Viewpoint and Church of Santa María de los Ángeles&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any waterfalls accessible by road bike in the Rionansa area?
A: Yes, some road cycling routes in the broader Rionansa area can lead you near natural attractions like waterfalls. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/994831" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lamiña Waterfalls&lt;/a&gt; are a notable natural highlight that might be incorporated into a cycling itinerary, offering a refreshing stop.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Rionansa?
A: The road cycling routes in Rionansa are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 40 reviews. Cyclists frequently praise the region's mountainous terrain, the scenic river valleys, and the low traffic density, which combine to create an enjoyable and challenging experience.

Q: Are there routes that explore the Saja-Besaya Natural Park?
A: Rionansa is located near the Saja-Besaya Natural Park, the largest protected area in Cantabria. While specific routes may vary, many cycling paths border or traverse parts of this park, allowing you to experience its vast deciduous forests and rich biodiversity. These routes offer a mix of paved and unpaved surfaces, catering to different cycling preferences.

Q: Can I find routes that offer coastal views from Rionansa?
A: Given Cantabria's unique blend of mountain and coastal environments, some routes from Rionansa extend towards the coast. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31914553" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Vicente de la Barquera – Oyambre Natural Park loop from El Barcenal&lt;/a&gt; offers a chance to experience coastal views and explore areas like Oyambre Natural Park, combining mountain and sea landscapes.

Q: Are there options for family-friendly road cycling in Rionansa?
A: While many routes are challenging, the region does offer some gentler, rolling routes that can be suitable for families or those looking for a more relaxed ride. These routes often provide fantastic views around the edges of the Picos de Europa or within the river valleys, focusing more on scenic enjoyment than strenuous climbing.
tips: One of the great wonders of geology that houses an unusual diversity of speleothems characterized by their beauty and abundance. In addition, the cave preserves an exceptional heritage of industrial archeology derived from the exploitation of zinc and lead that, from the mid-19th century to the end of the 1970s, was active in the Sierra de Arnero. A tourist visit lasts one hour but there is also the option of doing a 2.5 km route where you can feel like a speleologist for a day exploring the cave galleries in their natural state.

From here there are good views of the San Vicente estuary. The Escudo River runs through the western part of Cantabria and flows into the Cantabrian Sea through the San Vicente de la Barquera estuary.

With 500 meters in length, the bridge connects the town across the length and breadth of the San Vicente estuary. The first bridge was made of wood; then a stone one was built in the 15th century. On this, the current one was finished, during the reign of Carlos III. It represents the most traditional access road to reach San Vicente de la Barquera. When it was built in medieval times, it was listed as one of the most impressive works of naval engineering. There are privileged views of the estuary.

The Oyambre Natural Park has an extension of 57 square kilometers and consists of enormous beaches surrounded by an extensive forest mass. The Picos de Europa mountain range in the background is impressive. It is a territory of great ornithological richness, located mainly in its estuaries and marshes.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this road cycling loop?
faqs.answer: This challenging racebike loop is approximately 46.8 kilometers long and typically takes around 2 hours and 46 minutes to complete. However, given its 'difficult' rating and significant elevation gain, actual times may vary based on your fitness level and how often you stop to enjoy the views.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is notably difficult, featuring rugged, twisting roads with substantial elevation gains. While most surfaces are paved, some sections may be unpaved and challenging, occasionally requiring you to dismount and push your bike. It demands very good fitness.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access this cycling route?
faqs.answer: As this is a loop, you can start at various points. Parking is generally available near the Cueva de El Soplao or within the vicinity of the Oyambre Natural Park. Look for designated parking areas for visitors to these attractions.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: The route offers exceptionally diverse and beautiful scenery. You'll enjoy extensive views of the Sierra de Peña Sagra and Picos de Europa mountain ranges, the Nansa valley, and the Cantabrian Sea. Within Oyambre Natural Park, expect panoramic views of large cliffs, expansive beaches like Oyambre and El Merón, and important estuaries.

faqs.question: Are there any specific regulations or permits required for cycling in Oyambre Natural Park?
faqs.answer: Oyambre Natural Park is a protected area. While general road cycling is permitted on public roads, it's always advisable to check for any specific regulations regarding cycling within the park's protected zones, especially off-road. There are typically no entrance fees for general access to the park's public areas.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginners or family cycling?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as 'difficult' and requires 'very good fitness' due to its demanding terrain and significant elevation gains. It is not recommended for beginners or family cycling with young children. More moderate routes exist in the broader region, but this specific loop is designed for experienced road cyclists.

faqs.question: What are the best times of year to cycle this loop?
faqs.answer: The Cantabrian region is popular for cycling, and generally, spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures and fewer crowds. Summer can also be good, but be prepared for potentially warmer weather. Always check the local weather forecast before heading out, as conditions can change, especially in mountainous and coastal areas.

faqs.question: What points of interest will I see on this loop?
faqs.answer: Along the route, you'll pass by several notable highlights. These include the impressive Cueva de El Soplao, scenic Views of the San Vicente estuary (and nice bar), the historic Puente de la Maza, and the beautiful Oyambre Natural Park itself.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While dogs are generally allowed in many outdoor areas, specific regulations may apply within Oyambre Natural Park, especially concerning protected wildlife and sensitive ecosystems. If you plan to bring a dog, it's best to keep them on a leash and ensure you adhere to any local signage or park rules to protect the natural environment.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route briefly intersects with sections of other established paths. You'll find it crosses parts of the [CIMA CA17] El Soplao * La Florida, Northern Route (Cantabria), Transcantábrica, and Camino Lebaniego for short stretches.

faqs.question: Are there places to find food or drinks along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the highlight Views of the San Vicente estuary (and nice bar) specifically mentions a nice bar, indicating opportunities for refreshments. Additionally, the towns and villages you pass through or near, such as San Vicente de la Barquera, will likely offer cafes and pubs.

waypoints.name: Views of the San Vicente estuary (and nice bar)
